item.title: South African Stock Index Is Heading for Worst Month Since 2008
item.description: Johannesburg’s benchmark stock index is heading for its worst month in almost two decades, reeling from a double hit as the Iran war saps demand for emerging-market assets while plunging precious-metal prices weigh on the country’s miners.

item.title: WTO Talks End With No Deal to Extend E-Commerce Tariff Ban
item.description: The World Trade Organization failed to extend a ban on e-commerce tariffs at its 14th ministerial conference, as at least one member opposed US efforts to make the moratorium permanent.

item.title: Australia and India Plan More Trade Talks in Coming Weeks
item.description: Australia and India will meet for trade talks in the next few weeks, Trade Minister Don Farrell said Monday, as the government seeks to conclude another deal with New Delhi after an agreement with the European Union.

item.title: Inflexion Raises €4.5 Billion for Buyout Fund in Just Six Months
item.description: European private equity firm Inflexion has raised €4.5 billion ($4.7 billion) for its latest buyout fund in six months, demonstrating the depth of demand for mid-market funds that focus on the continent amid a still-tough fundraising market.

item.title: Glencore, Canada Near Deal to Save Threatened Copper Facility
item.description: The governments of Canada and Quebec are pushing to save the country’s only copper smelter after Glencore Plc threatened to shut it down over new pollution-control requirements.

item.title: FTSE 100 Set to Fall on Iran War Escalation Worries
BYD Signals It’s Confident Exports Will Beat 2026 Target By 15%
item.description: BYD Co. signaled to analysts that exports this year will probably beat its previous target by 15%, according to people familiar with the matter, as the world’s biggest electric vehicle maker leans on growth in overseas markets to counter a slump in domestic sales.

item.title: New Zealand Explores Swapping IEA Tickets to Secure Fuel Supply
item.description: New Zealand is considering using options it holds with the International Energy Agency as an insurance policy against any future squeeze on fuel supplies.

item.title: RBA Seen Lifting Rate to 17-Year High on Oil Shock, Westpac Says
item.description: Australia’s central bank will likely raise its benchmark rate three more times this year, taking it to more than a 17-year high amid the war in the Middle East and signs that rising fuel costs are feeding into prices, according to Westpac Banking Corp.’s Luci Ellis.

item.title: Chicago Wheat Climbs on Iran War Escalation, Weather Risks
item.description: Chicago wheat futures rose — heading for a fourth gain in five sessions — as the Iran war threatens to further push up energy and fertilizer costs for farmers and poor weather hampers US fields.

item.title: Oil Market Faces Shock as War Widens, Ex-US Energy Official Says
item.description: The global oil market is “in for a shock” as the Middle East conflict will likely extend for a prolonged period with Iran leveraging control of the Strait of Hormuz, said Randa Fahmy, a former US associate deputy energy secretary.

item.title: Israel to Hold Rates, Lower Growth Forecast as Iran War Persists
item.description: Israel’s central bank is set to hold interest rates steady for a second consecutive time as the US-Israeli attacks on Iran keep fueling global and domestic inflation concerns.

item.title: Thai PM Readies New Cabinet as Energy Crisis Threatens Growth
item.description: Thai Prime Minister Anutin Charnvirakul is close to unveiling a new cabinet lineup, handing him more power to deal with the challenges posed by the Middle East crisis, which is threatening the Southeast Asian nation’s growth outlook.

item.title: Australian Fund With $123 Billion Weighs Private Credit Boost
item.description: Pension and wealth manager Colonial First State is looking to floating-rate debt and inflation-protected bonds to navigate rising inflation and slowing growth caused by higher energy prices.

item.title: Israel Approves Revised War Budget Extending Reliance on Debt
item.description: Israel’s parliament approved a 2026 state budget with a large defense supplement to cover the war with Iran, to be funded by additional borrowing and cuts in civilian spending.

item.title: Chinese Cargoes Hint at Fuels Relief for Neighbors Despite Curbs
item.description: China has exported cargoes of diesel and other fuels to energy-starved countries across Southeast Asia over the weekend, in what appears to be a signal of support despite export curbs imposed earlier this month.

item.title: Will the Ayatollahs Chicken Out Is the New TACO
item.description: Iran’s leaders have more incentives to keep the conflict going than Trump does.

item.title: Why Young People Can’t Find a Job in London Anymore
item.description: Thanim Rahman had every reason to smile when his LinkedIn profile picture was taken in September 2025.

item.title: Swiss Real Estate Scion Lauber Joins Rush to Access Retail Cash
item.description: Infinitas Capital, a Swiss family office run by real estate heir Robin Lauber, plans to launch a new investment company that will seek a listing on Nasdaq Stockholm.

item.title: Investors Balk at Terms in JPMorgan’s $7.2 Billion Sealed Air Debt Deal
item.description: Banks led by JPMorgan Chase & Co. are facing pushback on the terms of a $7.2 billion debt deal to fund Clayton, Dubilier & Rice’s takeover of Bubble Wrap maker Sealed Air Corp., according to people familiar with the matter.

item.title: Indian Rupee Surges as RBI’s Cap on Positions Spurs Dollar Sales
item.description: India’s rupee posted its biggest gain since February, rebounding from record lows after a central bank move aimed at curbing speculation triggered a rush among lenders to sell dollars.

item.title: Robot Maker Inovance Is Said to Hire Banks for Up to $2 Billion Hong Kong Listing
item.description: Industrial robot maker Shenzhen Inovance Technology Co. has picked banks for a Hong Kong listing that could raise as much as $2 billion, according to people familiar with the matter.

item.title: RBI Steps In as Rupee Hits Fresh Low Amid Iran War
item.description: Lenders are urging the central bank to rethink the new rules.

item.title: Government Bonds Rally Around the World on Slowdown Concerns
item.description: Sovereign bonds are rising around the world as concern the Middle East conflict will derail global economic growth revives demand for beaten-down government debt.

item.title: South Korea Weighs First Driving Curbs in 35 Years on Oil Crunch
item.description: South Korea may widen restrictions on driving to include the general public if oil prices breach $120 a barrel as it seeks to cushion the impact of the Iran war on its energy supplies, the country’s finance minister said.

item.title: New Zealand to Fill Markets Gap With Return of NZX Index Futures
item.description: New Zealand will offer futures based on the S&P/NZX 20 stock index starting in late April, addressing a longstanding gap in the nation’s capital markets.

item.title: China Increases Overseas Investment Quota by Most Since 2021
item.description: China raised the cap on institutional investors’ overseas securities purchases by the most since 2021, aiming to advance financial opening and meet greater domestic demand for offshore investment.

item.title: BOJ Summary Displays Hawkish Tilt With Debate on Size of Hike
item.description: The Bank of Japan’s policy board presented a hawkish posture in a summary of opinions aired during their meeting earlier this month, with one member hinting at the possibility of having to respond to the Middle East conflict with a bigger rate hike than those recently undertaken.

item.title: DeepSeek Probes Hours-Long AI Outage After Users Report Errors
item.description: DeepSeek’s chatbot suffered a major outage of more than seven hours overnight in China, prompting the AI pioneer to deploy several updates to try and fix the issue.

item.title: RBA Opens Door to Board Member Speeches in Transparency Push
item.description: The Reserve Bank of Australia’s monetary policy board members will begin delivering public speeches this year, in a shift aimed at boosting transparency around rate decisions and the views behind them.
